topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The delivery notification blinked on my phone, a stark intrusion into the quiet afternoon. A package, addressed to the previous resident, sat on my doorstep. I hesitated, my hand hovering over the cardboard. The building felt too still; the usual background hum of the neighborhood seemed muted, expectant. I circled the entrance twice, my gaze sweeping the darkened windows of the adjacent units. A prickle of unease crawled up my neck. It wasn't just mail; it was an invitation into someone else's private space, and I wasn't sure I wanted to be the one to open it. I retreated inside, locking the deadbolt with unnecessary force, the click echoing loudly in the sudden silence.

[story 2]

I watched the package sit there, a dull brown rectangle against the grey concrete. Every shadow in the hallway seemed to shift with an unnatural speed. My breath hitched, shallow and quick in my chest. I pulled my collar higher around my neck, a purely instinctive gesture of self-protection. Who would want this? And why would they leave it here, addressed to someone long gone? I moved toward the door, my steps measured and deliberate, each footfall on the worn floorboards sounding amplified in the stillness. My knuckles were white as I reached for the box, the paper feeling strangely cold beneath my fingertips. A low murmur of apprehension vibrated in my throat.

[story 3]

The sound of the delivery truck fading away left an oppressive quiet. I stood by the mail slot, my jaw tight. This wasn't just junk mail; this was a tangible piece of the past someone else had left behind. My eyes darted from the package to the fire escape, then back again, cataloging every detail of the surroundings. A tension coiled in my shoulders, making my spine feel rigid. I didn't want to engage, didn't want to acknowledge the contents. Instead, I took a slow, deep breath, trying to steady the frantic rhythm of my pulse. I picked up the package with two fingers, careful not to let my skin brush against the rough surface, and carried it toward the relative safety of my apartment, every muscle tensed for the journey.
